risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) of the project using a personal computer before subject recruitment. Subjects in both groups... [were assigned] after... the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-based random number generation process used before recruitment, which is a proper random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "Eligible selected subjects signed the consent form and completed the baseline measures ... before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group. The random numbers for group assignment were generated by the research assistant (not the counselors) ... before subject recruitment." Comment: This explicitly describes a valid concealment mechanism (sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes) opened only after enrollment and baseline assessment, which would prevent the counselor/recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blinded randomized controlled trial (RCT)" and "before the counselor opened a serially numbered, opaque, and sealed envelope (SNOSE) to reveal the random assignment of each smoker to A1 or A2 group." Comment: The study explicitly says only single-blinded, and the allocation was revealed to the counselor after enrollment, so participants/personnel delivering the intervention were not successfully blinded to group assignment. Because the intervention was different amounts of free NRT plus counseling and the outcomes included self-reported smoking cessation and related behavioral outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes and co-interventions, creating performance bias.

Quote: "The main outcome was self-reported 7-day point prevalence quit rate at 6 months" and "At 6 months and 12 months follow-up assessment ... An independent interviewer, who was unaware of the subject's group allocation, carried out the 6 and 12 months follow-up interview." Comment: The outcome assessor for follow-up interviews was blinded, but the main and several secondary outcomes were self-reported smoking cessation/reduction measures, meaning the participant was effectively the assessor for these subjective outcomes. Because participants were not reported as blinded and the study relied on self-report for the primary outcome and other subjective outcomes, knowledge of allocation could bias outcome measurement; the blinded interviewer does not remove this risk for self-reported outcomes.

Quote: "All the 716 subjects who attended the clinic underwent screening for inclusion... Of the 598 eligible subjects, 36 refused to participate, and 562 were available for randomization" ... "We conducted all analyses on an intention-to-treat basis. Follow-up variables with missing data were set to their baseline values (e.g., current smoker)." Comment: The paper explicitly reports ITT with baseline-value imputation for missing follow-up data, and the randomized groups are analyzed with denominators of 284 and 278 throughout the outcome tables. No per-arm differential loss or large total loss at the analysed time points is reported in the results shown; the available follow-up data appear sufficiently handled for this domain, and no threshold-crossing attrition signal is communicated in the paper excerpts provided.
